did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an attention-based Vision Transformer (ViT), a deep learning architecture, to classify skin cancer (benign vs. malignant) using hyperspectral imaging data. The study evaluates the performance of this AI model on a dataset of 76 hyperspectral images of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work evaluating a parallel Vision Transformer architecture for skin cancer detection using hyperspectral imaging. It describes specific methods including the use of Edge and Cloud computing hardware (GPUs, Jetson boards), details the dataset used (76 hyperspectral images), and reports new results comparing processing times and power consumption across different computing paradigms.

title: Edge and cloud computing approaches in the early diagnosis of skin cancer with attention‑based vision transformer through hyperspectral imaging
brief_summary: This study evaluates the feasibility of using Edge and Cloud computing architectures for the early diagnosis of skin cancer via hyperspectral imaging with an attention-based Vision Transformer. The model was trained and tested on a dataset of 76 hyperspectral images of benign and malignant skin lesions collected from 61 subjects. The researchers compared processing times and power consumption across various hardware platforms, including low-power Edge GPUs, desktop GPUs, and Cloud GPU clusters. Results indicated that Cloud solutions offered the lowest processing times, while Edge-based GPUs demonstrated the lowest energy consumption. The authors conclude that Edge computing represents the optimal trade-off for developing portable skin cancer diagnostic instruments where energy efficiency is prioritized over extreme speed.

lesion_types_studied.details: 40 benign skin lesions in the dataset (53% of total), including benign melanocytic tumors (45% of total).
36 malignant skin lesions in the dataset (47% of total), including malignant epithelial tumors (32% of total).
Mentioned as a category of skin cancer and implied by 'malignant melanocytic' tumors in the dataset distribution.
Mentioned in the introduction as comprising 75-80% of non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) lesions, but not explicitly listed as a specific class in the experimental dataset taxonomy.

Dataset taxonomy splits into melanocytic and epidermal tumors. Benign melanocytic (45%) and malignant epithelial (32%) are the most prevalent.

all_task_types.details: The paper uses an attention-based Vision Transformer to classify hyperspectral images of skin lesions into benign or malignant categories. The dataset consists of 76 hyperspectral images with ground truth labels for benign (40 images) and malignant (36 images) lesions. The output is described as a 'thematic map' where each pixel is assigned a color representing a specific tissue type or lesion condition (benign/malignant), which constitutes a pixel-wise classification task.

datasets_used.name: Hyperspectral Skin Lesion Dataset (Las Palmas)
datasets_used.description: A dataset of hyperspectral images of skin lesions acquired from 116 subjects at the Doctor Negrin University Hospital and the Materno Infantil University Hospital Complex in Las Palmas de Gran Canaria, Spain. The final database consists of 76 images after removing 55 images from challenging areas. The data was captured using a Cubert UHD 185 snapshot camera with a spectral range of 450-950 nm. Ground truth labels were assigned by pathologists and dermatologists via histological assessment and manual segmentation.

use.training: The dataset was used to train the attention-based Vision Transformer (ViT) model. The paper notes it is a 'small-size dataset' and that data augmentation techniques were adopted.
use.testing: The dataset was used to test the model's performance in terms of classification accuracy and to evaluate processing times and power consumption across Edge and Cloud hardware.
use.other: The dataset was used to compare Edge and Cloud computing paradigms for portable skin cancer diagnostic instruments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The study focuses on evaluating processing times, power consumption, and energy dissipation across different hardware architectures (Edge vs. Cloud) using deterministic measurements rather than statistical inference.

ViT Classification Performance.notes: Results from Table 2. The dataset consists of 76 hyperspectral images (50x50 pixels, 125 bands) from 61 subjects. Ground truth labels were assigned by pathologists/dermatologists.

Processing Time (Single Image Inference).notes: Results from Table 3. Values represent the time to process a single hyperspectral image. Jetson Nano Eco Mode is slower than Full Power Mode due to power limiting.

Cloud Solution Total Processing Time (In.metric: Total processing time (s) at different upload bands
Cloud Solution Total Processing Time (In.notes: Results from Table 5. Total time = Processing time (Table 3) + Transfer time (Table 4). Transfer time depends on upload bandwidth.

Energy Dissipation.notes: Values for Jetson Nano (Full Power) and others are inferred from the text stating Edge GPUs have energy consumption 'one order of magnitude lower' than Cloud solutions and specific relative comparisons. Exact values for all boards in Table 6 were not fully legible in the provided text chunks, but the Jetson Nano Eco Mode is highlighted as having the lowest energy consumption. The text explicitly states: 'Edge solutions based on GPUs emerged as the best trade-off... Jetson boards... values are one order of magnitude lower than the best cloud-based solutions.'

architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported; the paper states that to the best of the authors' knowledge, no previous ViT architecture was trained on medical hyperspectral images, implying the model was trained from scratch on the specific dataset.

architecture_details.backbone: Vision Transformer with multi-head self-attention (MSA) and multilayer perceptron (MLP)
architecture_details.preprocessing: Calibration using white/dark reference images; removal of first 4 and last 5 bands (125 to 116 bands); smoothing filter with sample window of 5; min-max normalization to [0,1].
architecture_details.bands_reduction: Three cascaded convolutional blocks, each with 3x3 filters, normalization, and ReLU activation, reducing bands from 116 to 14.
architecture_details.patching: Convolution operation transforming input H x W x C to H x P x P x C; patches unrolled into 1-D arrays and projected into latent space.
architecture_details.transformer_encoder: Composed of multi-head self-attention (MSA), multilayer perceptron (MLP), and normalization. MSA generates Key (K), Query (Q), and Value (V) vectors. Attention map computed via softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d))V.
architecture_details.implementation: Parallel implementation using CUDA language, cuBLAS library (cublasSgemm for matrix multiplications), and cuDNN. Serial version developed for CPU reference.

training_details.data_augmentation: Adopted in the original ViT development (details not specified in this paper).

classification_or_output_head.details: An MLP outside the ViT combines the output of all transformer encoders to produce the final classification. The class is assigned based on the perceptron with the maximum value, found using the cublasIsamax routine.

preprocessing_techniques: Calibration using white and dark reference images (Eq. 1: (RI - DI) / (WI - DI))
Removal of the first 4 and last 5 spectral bands (reducing from 125 to 116 bands) due to poor sensor spectral response
Smoothing filter with a sample window of 5 to reduce spectral noise
Min-max normalization of spectral values to the range [0, 1]
Bands reduction via three cascaded convolutional blocks (3x3 filters, normalization, ReLU activation) reducing bands from 116 to 14
Patching via convolution operation transforming input shape H x W x C to H x P x P x C
Unrolling patches into 1-D arrays and projecting into latent space
Integration of position embedding and class token into patches

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper states that data augmentation techniques were adopted for the small-size dataset, but it does not specify the particular methods, probabilities, or settings used. It notes that these techniques were part of the original ViT development rather than detailed in this specific study.

conclusions: Cloud GPU solutions provide the lowest processing times for skin cancer classification using hyperspectral imaging compared to Edge and serial CPU solutions.
Edge-based GPU solutions (specifically NVIDIA Jetson boards) demonstrate the lowest energy consumption, with dissipated energy values one order of magnitude lower than the best cloud-based solutions.
Edge solutions based on GPUs represent the optimal trade-off between processing time and energy consumption for developing portable skin cancer diagnostic instruments.
The attention-based Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture was successfully implemented in parallel using CUDA, cuBLAS, and cuDNN, enabling evaluation across diverse hardware architectures.

The model was trained on a small-size dataset of 76 hyperspectral images, and the authors acknowledge that generalization capabilities could be improved by acquiring additional data or using transfer learning with pre-trained architectures.

Jetson Nano boards in both Full Power and Eco modes are identified as innovative solutions for portable instrumentation due to their low energy dissipation, despite having processing times six times slower than Cloud GPUs.
Direct comparison with state-of-the-art works is challenging due to differences in algorithm structures, computational complexities, and data dimensionality.

Future work should focus on developing user-friendly interfaces for data visualization and evaluating other classification algorithms to determine the impact of data dimensionality on the Edge/Cloud trade-off.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small dataset of only 76 hyperspectral images from 61 subjects, which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the classification results.
The model was trained from scratch without pre-training, as no prior ViT architectures were available for medical hyperspectral images, increasing the risk of overfitting on the small dataset.

Classification performance is highly imbalanced; while benign melanocytic lesions achieved 84% accuracy, malignant epithelial lesions had poor performance (41% accuracy, 5% precision), indicating potential bias or difficulty in detecting specific malignant subtypes.
The dataset distribution (53% benign, 47% malignant) reflects the actual population prevalence, but the small sample size per class (e.g., only 36 malignant lesions) restricts robust validation.
Direct comparison with state-of-the-art works is noted as challenging due to differences in algorithm structures, computational complexities, and data dimensionality.

The study focuses on computational efficiency (processing time, energy) rather than clinical diagnostic accuracy metrics like sensitivity, specificity, or AUC, limiting its direct clinical relevance assessment.
Data augmentation techniques were adopted but not specified in detail, making it difficult to assess the extent of mitigation for the small dataset size.
The hardware evaluation includes a wide range of devices (Jetson Nano, RTX 2080/4090, A16, CPU), but the results are deterministic measurements without statistical variance or confidence intervals.
The study acknowledges that generalization capabilities could be improved by acquiring additional data or using transfer learning, which was not feasible at the time of writing.
Privacy concerns regarding patient data are cited as a motivation for Edge computing, but the study does not evaluate data security protocols or compliance with regulations like HIPAA/GDPR.
